comments.text: The proof by Erdős combined with slightly more recent knowledge provides the required lower bound on $H(n)$ for infinitely many $n$, modulo the exact value of the constant $c$. Let me explain. Let $\omega^*(n)$ count the number of primes $p$ such that $p-1$ divides $n$. By Proposition $10$ in this 1983 paper by Adleman, Pomerance and Rumely (see page $201$, or page $29$ of the pdf-file), there exists an absolute constant $c > 0$ such that there are infinitely many $n$ with $\omega^*(n) > n^{c/\log \log n}$. So let $n$ be a large enough integer for which $m > n^{c/\log \log n}$ primes $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_m$ exist with $p_i - 1 | n$. With $k < l = H(n)$ such that $\gcd(k^n-1, l^n-1) = 1$, we then must have that $kl$ is divisible by the product $p_1p_2 \cdots p_m$ due to Fermat's little theorem. By noting that this product $p_1p_2\cdots p_m$ is, by the prime number theorem, larger than $e^{2m}$ if $n$ (and therefore $m$) is large enough, we get \begin{align*} H(n) &> \sqrt{kl} \\ &\ge \sqrt{p_1p_2 \cdots p_m} \\ &> e^m \\ &> \exp[n^{c/\log \log n}], \end{align*}as promised. By the way, since $\omega^*(n)$ is at most the number of divisors of $n$, which is in turn upper bounded by $n^{(\log 2 + o(1))/\log \log n}$, it is tempting to think that the optimal $c$ could be $\log 2$. But I certainly have no idea and this is pure speculation. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
It may be interesting to link this problem to the related problem [\#770]. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
